coredata.dc:description: Biofouling of underwater equipment caused by marine organisms is a major problem in the marine industry. Although hydrogel exhibits excellent antifouling properties, its mechanical performance greatly limits its practical application. In this work, a suitable marine antifouling hydrogel (PSM) was designed utilizing a polyethylene glycol–modified MXene (Ti3C2Tx-PEG) toughening mechanism and a silica loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SiO2@N-CDs) reinforcement strategy to enhance the durability of the hydrogel. The PSM hydrogel exhibited a fracture strength of up to 1.62 MPa, elongation of 1734 %, and toughness of 17.7 MJ/m3. Furthermore, the PSM hydrogel exhibited excellent photothermal antibacterial properties, with antibacterial rates against E. coli and S. aureus reaching 100%. After 180 days of immersion in seawater, the PSM hydrogel morphology remained stable. Because of its long-term durability and effective antifouling properties, our PSM hydrogel can be developed for use in marine ship coatings.

head.ce:title: Photothermal synergistic antifouling and high-toughness hydrogel based on Ti3C2Tx-PEG for marine coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofouling of underwater equipment caused by marine organisms is a major problem in the marine industry. Although hydrogel exhibits excellent antifouling properties, its mechanical performance greatly limits its practical application. In this work, a suitable marine antifouling hydrogel (PSM) was designed utilizing a polyethylene glycol–modified MXene (Ti3C2Tx-PEG) toughening mechanism and a silica loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SiO2@N-CDs) reinforcement strategy to enhance the durability of the hydrogel. The PSM hydrogel exhibited a fracture strength of up to 1.62 MPa, elongation of 1734 %, and toughness of 17.7 MJ/m3. Furthermore, the PSM hydrogel exhibited excellent photothermal antibacterial properties, with antibacterial rates against E. coli and S. aureus reaching 100%. After 180 days of immersion in seawater, the PSM hydrogel morphology remained stable. Because of its long-term durability and effective antifouling properties, our PSM hydrogel can be developed for use in marine ship coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene glycol (PEG) hydrogels are characterized by their water solubility and lubricating properties, which make their surfaces resistant to biological adhesion and growth [1–3]. When coated on the surfaces of ships or other marine facilities, they effectively prevent fouling by marine organisms, making them potentially valuable for antifouling applications in the marine industry [4–6].

However, hydrogels have limitations such as low strength after water absorption and poor adhesion to the substrate [7,8]. These limitations greatly restrict their application in marine antifouling engineering. Currently, the direct application of PEG hydrogel materials as marine antifouling coatings remains under exploration and research. To develop effective marine antifouling materials, it is necessary to combine PEG hydrogels with other antifouling components or develop special designs to improve their mechanical stability and durability [9–11]. Researchers have conducted extensive studies on such polymer materials in recent years to create more environmentally friendly and efficient solutions for marine antifouling.

With the rise and development of nanomaterials, a number of studies on composite hydrogels have been reported [12–14]. The specificity and unsaturated bonds on the surfaces of inorganic nanoparticles allow them to function as crosslinking sites in hydrogels, thereby increasing the mechanical strength of the materials. Silica (SiO2) is widely used in biomedical and biotechnology hydrogel materials because of its controllable size, [15] stable characteristics, [16] and special optical properties [17]. Our research group loaded fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) onto SiO2 (SiO2@N-CDs) using an in suit polymerization method to obtain a SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel membrane system [18]. SiO2 increased the breaking strength of the PEG hydrogel. N-CDs fluorescence tracing was used to reveal the distribution of SiO2 in the PEG hydrogel. The experiment showed that adding 1 wt% SiO2 resulted in the best performance for the SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel. Compared with the PEG hydrogel, the SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel had a higher breaking strength of 1.3 MPa, a higher storage modulus, and a lower equilibrium swelling rate. This result is attributed to its ability to function as a multifunctional crosslinking agent, which increased the crosslinking density and rigidity of the system, thereby limiting the movement of PEG chains. Our research also showed that the fracture elongation of the SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel was only half that of the PEG hydrogel, resulting in a decrease in the toughness of the hydrogel. The toughness of hydrogels is particularly important for marine antifouling coatings, [19] which require long-term stability and durability. Hydrogels with good toughness can withstand repeated stretching, compression, and twisting without fracturing, thereby increasing their service life [20]. Higher toughness means that these gels can withstand larger loads without fracturing. As a result, further modification of the SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel was required to improve its toughness.

MXene is a new type of material consisting of two-dimensional layered metal carbides and nitrides [21]. They are highly valued for their excellent conductivity, [22] large specific surface area, [23] and high mechanical properties [24]. Furthermore, they can be combined with materials such as hydrogels, metals, and ceramics to enhance the performance of composite materials [25]. The layered structure of MXene can absorb energy via relative sliding between layers under stress, thereby improving energy dissipation [26]. As a nanoscale filler, MXene layers distributed in a hydrogel matrix can effectively restrict crack propagation and improve fracture toughness [27]. For example, Wu et al. used a glycerol/water binary solvent as a dispersion medium to incorporate MXene nanosheets into polymer networks of dopamine-grafted sodium alginate (Alg-DA), phenylboronic acid–grafted sodium alginate (Alg-PBA), and polyacrylamide (PAM) [28]. The resulting MXene nanocomposite organic hydrogel exhibited self-healing capability and good self-adhesiveness. Cai et al. proposed a PAM hydrogel modified by vinyl-functionalized silica nanoparticles. The hydrogel contained a hybrid of one-dimensional polypyrrole nanowires (PPyNWs) and two-dimensional MXene nanosheets [29]. This structure endowed the hydrogel with skin-like functionality and sensitivity, with a stretching performance of up to 2800%. Using a solution blending method with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the base material, Li et al. successfully prepared MXene@Ag-III/PVA composite films. These composite films not only exhibited excellent thermal conductivity and electromagnetic interference shielding capability but also possessed outstanding mechanical and thermal properties. The Young’s modulus of this composite reached 3.8 GPa, and the thermal stability index reached 175.3°C [30]. Dai et al. successfully developed a novel MXene hydrogel with a dual physical crosslinking structure, demonstrating high toughness, low hysteresis, and excellent resistance to drying and swelling. This hydrogel maintained outstanding conductivity, sensitivity, and long-term stability in harsh environments, including underwater, acidic, alkaline, saline, and alcohol solutions [31]. Wang et al. studied the effect of the ratio between MXene and graphene oxide (rGO) on capacitors and found that when the mass ratio of MXene to rGO was 4:1, it had the maximum area specific capacitance [32]. Wang et al. has prepared a composite film with MXene/SiO2 particles, which enables the material to remain stable under tension or bending [33]. Lv et al. developed a double-crosslinked hydrogel containing SiO2 and MXene and found that modified SiO2 prevents the self-stacking of Ti3C2Tx material through its electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding formation [34].

Because MXene can effectively improve the toughness of hydrogels, we used MXene (Ti3C2Tx) as the filler in the SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel. However, the stability of the hydrogel with Ti3C2Tx added was poor, obvious delamination occurred, and the hydrogel performance did not improve. Therefore, the uniform dispersion of Ti3C2Tx into SiO2@N-CDs/PEG to improve PEG hydrogel toughness has become an urgent problem to be solved.

Herein, we prepared single-layer Ti3C2Tx and then modified its surface with PEG to obtain Ti3C2Tx-PEG to improve the stability of the material system in solution. Finally, Ti3C2Tx-PEG was introduced into the hydrogel system via in situ polymerization, resulting in the Ti3C2Tx-PEG/SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel (PSM hydrogel) with high toughness, mechanical strength, and antibacterial properties.

Organ-shaped Ti3C2Tx (MXene) was purchased from Beijing Beike New Materials Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing). Dimethyl sulfoxide [DMSO; analytical grade (AR)], sodium chloroacetate, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, AR), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), PEG (number average molecular weight (M n) = 2,000, AR), polypropylene glycol (PPG, Mn = 2000, AR), isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, AR), and 1,4-butanediol (BDO, AR) were purchased from Aladdin (China). Butyl acetate, cyclohexanone, and xylenes were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Co., Ltd (China). Polyether ternary alcohol (HSH300, Mn = 3000) was obtained from Jiangsu Hai'an Petrochemical Plant (China). Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC25923) were purchased from Anolun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing). Diatom was obtained from the Institute of Oceanography (Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao, China). Seawater was taken from the Yellow Sea in Dalian, China. Deionized water and SiO2@N-CDs were prepared in our laboratory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Ti3C2Tx-PEG and SiO2@N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ti3C2Tx (5.0 g) was added to 125 mL of DMSO and stirred at a speed of 1000 rpm for 24 h. Deionized water (250 mL) was added to the mixture and sonicated for 30 min. The mixed solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min and then freeze-dried to obtain few-layer Ti3C2Tx. A 200 mL aqueous solution of few-layer Ti3C2Tx (1 mg/mL) was prepared, sonicated for 15 min, and stirred at 2000 rpm for 30 min to ensure a uniform dispersion. Sodium chloroacetate solution (100 mL, 10 mg/mL) was slowly added and stirred for 30 min. Then, sodium hydroxide (5 mol/mL) solution was added dropwise until the pH was 8, followed by 4 h of stirring. The mixture was transferred to a dialysis bag (Mw cut off: 3.5 kDa) and dialyzed for 48 h until the solution pH was neutral. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain Ti3C2Tx-COOH. A 200 mL Ti3C2Tx-COOH aqueous solution was prepared and sonicated for 15 min to ensure a uniform dispersion. The Ti3C2Tx-COOH solution (1 mg/mL) was slowly added to a PEG solution (200 mL, 0.3 mg/mL) and stirred at 30°C for 15 min. Then, EDC (100 mL, 1 mg/mL) and DMAP (20 mL, 1 mg/mL) were added, and the mixture was stirred for 3 h and dialyzed with deionized water for 48 h to remove unreacted PEG, EDC, and DMAP. The mixture was dried using a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain Ti3C2Tx-PEG (Fig. S1A).

TMAOH hydrolyzes in anhydrous ethanol to form TMA+ and OH–. TMA+ and SiO2 are combined together through electrostatic interactions to form SiO2@TMA+. Calcination of SiO2@TMA+ at 350°C, leads to the fracture and recombination of TMA+ organic components, resulting in the formation of SiO2@N-CDs [18].

The mixed solvent (Weight ratio of butyl acetate: xylene: cyclohexanone = 2:2:1) was dehydrated using 4 Å molecular sieves. PEG, PPG, and HSH330 were vacuum dehydrated (120°C, 3 h). IPDI (5 g) was added to 0.5 g of Ti3C2Tx-PEG and sonicated for 20 min to obtain Ti3C2Tx-PEG-NCO. SiO2@N-CDs-NCO was prepared using the same method. PEG (9 g), PPG (14 g), and HSH330 (6 g) were added to the mixed solvent (40 g) and stirred at 230 rpm for 30 min. The temperature was increased to 55°C and the dispersed Ti3C2Tx-PEG-NCO/SiO2@N-CDs-NCO solution was slowly added to the flask. The mixture was stirred for 15 min and the temperature was increased to 73°C for 3 h of reaction. The temperature was cooled to 55°C and 0.5 g of BDO was added to the flask. The mixture was stirred for 15 min, and then the temperature was increased to 73°C. The reaction was complete after 5 h, and the Ti3C2Tx-PEG/SiO2@N-CDs/PEG hydrogel was obtained after 24 h of wet curing (Fig. 1 ). Due to the key roles of PEG, SiO2@N-CDs, and MXene (Ti3C2Tx-PEG) in enhancing the antifouling and mechanical properties of the hydrogel, we combined the initials of these three materials to create a new name for this hydrogel—PSM. The Ti3C2Tx-PEG/SiO2@N-CDs content was controlled to obtain 1 wt%. The Ti3C2Tx-PEG content was varied (0 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 0.75 wt%, and 1 wt%) to prepare five groups of PSM hydrogels (PSM0, PSM0.25, PSM0.50, PSM0.75, and PSM1.0, respectively).

The mechanism of the hydrogel synthesis involved esterification of the –OH groups on PEG, PPG, HSH330, Ti3C2Tx-PEG, and SiO2@N-CDs with the –N=C=O group on IPDI. HSH330 has three –OH groups acting as crosslinking agents (Fig. S1B).

Observation of the Ti3C2Tx-PEG crystal composition was performed using X-ray diffractometry (XRD, D/MAX Ultima, Rigaku, Japan). The instrument used copper palladium, with a testing range of 10°–60°, step size of 0.02°, and time step of 5°/min. The interlayer spacing was calculated according to d = nλ/2sinθ, where n = 1 and λ = 0.15406 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, EQUINOX5, Bruker, Germany) was used to analyze the synthesized Ti3C2Tx-PEG and hydrogel. The scanning wavenumber range was 4000–650 cm−1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SUPRA-55, Carl Zeiss AG, Germany) was used to observe the micromorphology of the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheet and PSM hydrogel. Before examining the PSM hydrogel morphology, it was swelled for 24 h and then freeze-dried for 24 h in a vacuum freeze dryer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) was used to observe the morphological characteristics of Ti3C2Tx-PEG. The PSM hydrogel morphology was examined using a confocal laser–scanning microscope (CLSM, FV1200, Olympus, Japan). A contact angle instrument (JC2000) was used to analyze the water contact angle of the hydrogel. A zeta potential analyzer (ZS90, Malvern, Britain) was used to detect the numerical value of the surface potential of the samples. A Ti3C2Tx-PEG solution (50 μg/mL) was prepared and dispersed using a water bath ultrasonicator. A 1 mL sample was dripped into the electrochemical sample cell for testing, with each sample tested three times.

A 10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm PSM hydrogel was prepared, placed in an oven for 24 h to dry, and weighed as W0. The dried hydrogel was submerged in deionized water for 48 h, and the swollen hydrogel was weighed as Ws. Calculate the swelling degree according to equation (1) and the water content according to equation (2): [35]

Tensile testing was performed using a microcomputer-controlled electronic universal testing machine (UTM5105, China) with the PSM hydrogel cut into dumbbell shapes measuring 75 mm × 10 mm × 2 mm. The stretching rate was 50 mm/min, with an initial gauge length of 25 mm. Each sample was stretched three times. The toughness of the PSM hydrogel was calculated as the area covered by the stress–strain curve below the fracture point. A load–unload tensile experiment was performed on the PSM hydrogel. First, the PSM was subjected to a load tensile test at a rate of 50 mm/min. Based on the stress–strain curve, the maximum stretching rate of the PSM hydrogel was set to λmax = 7. Then, the PSM hydrogel was unloaded to its initial state. The toughness recovery rate was calculated as the rate of the dissipated energy at different recovery times divided by the dissipated energy (Uhys ) during the first load–unload test. Uhys was calculated from the area of the hysteresis loop between the load–unload curves according to equation (3): [36]
(3)

)
d ε
where σloading and σunloading represent the stress during the loading and unloading processes, respectively, and εf is the strain at fracture of the PSM hydrogel.

The PSM hydrogel was placed flat in a 24 well plate. An 808 nm near infrared (NIR) laser with a power density of 2.0 W/cm2 was used to vertically irradiate the hydrogel at a distance of 10 cm for 5 min. The laser irradiation was turned off and the PSM hydrogel was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then the laser was turned on again for the next irradiation. The entire experimental process was repeated five times, and the temperature changes of the PSM hydrogel were recorded during multiple photothermal cycles using an infrared thermal imaging camera.

E. coli and S. aureus were inoculated on solid culture media and cultivated at 37°C for 24 h. Single colonies were selected, transferred to 25 mL of liquid culture medium, and incubated on a shaker at 37°C for 12 h. The bacterial suspension was centrifuged for 20 min, the supernatant was discarded, and the pellet was washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) 2 or 3 times. The above bacterial suspension was diluted. The PSM hydrogel was immersed in 50 mL of the diluted bacterial suspension and then cultured for 24 h. A 1 mL sample of the cultured bacterial suspension was diluted 10 times. A 100 μL sample of the diluted solution was spread evenly onto agar plates and the plates were incubated in an incubator for 24 h. Photos were taken to document the growth of bacterial colonies.

Soak the PSM hydrogel in seawater and cultivate at 28 °C for 24 h. After cultivation, rinse the hydrogel with 50 mL of sterilized seawater, then dilute the solution to 10-6 times. Take 10 μL of the diluted solution and spread it onto 2216E medium. Place the medium in a biochemical incubator and incubate for 36 h. Use a colony counter to observe and count the bacterial colonies on the culture medium.

The plate counting method was used to evaluate the photothermal antibacterial effect of the PSM hydrogel on E. coli and S. aureus. First, the bacterial suspension was diluted to a concentration of 5 × 107 CFU/mL. Then, 0.5 mL of the bacterial suspension was added to a 96 well plate. The PSM hydrogel was added at a concentration of 20 µg/mL and coincubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Next, the samples were irradiated using a NIR laser for 5 min, with the laser switched on and off every 30 s throughout this period. After irradiation, the bacterial solution was placed in a shaking incubator at 37°C with a shaking speed of 120 rpm for 4 h. After incubation, the bacterial solution was diluted with physiological saline at equal multiples. A 100 μL sample of the diluted solution was evenly spread on agar plates and incubated in an incubator for 24 h. The plates were then removed for photography to document the growth of bacterial colonies.

The PSM hydrogel was immersed in seawater with a concentration of 0.3 μmol/mL of diatom seawater and cultured under light conditions at 22°C for 24 h. Then, the hydrogel was gently rinsed with sterile seawater. Subsequently, it was placed in a test tube containing 45 mL of acetone solution (90 wt%). To prevent chlorophyll decomposition, 1 mL of 1% MgCO3 was added, and the test tube was sealed in a dark environment at 4°C for 24 h. The supernatant was then removed and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. The absorbance intensity was measured at 630, 645, 663, and 750 nm using ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV 3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). The chlorophyll-a concentration and adhesion rate (R) were calculated according to equations (4) and (5), respectively: [37]

where OD630, OD645, and OD663 are the corrected absorbance values, which are the differences between the chlorophyll absorbance values at 630, 645, and 663 nm and the absorbance value at 750 nm. Nb is the amount of diatoms adhered to the coating and Nc is the amount of diatoms adhered to the blank control group.

According to the national standard GB/T 9286–2012, the coating was cut into a 6 × 6 grid, with each grid measuring 3 mm × 3 mm. Then, a 25 mm wide silicone tape was tightly applied to the crosshatch area. After standing for 5 min, the tape was peeled off, and the peeling of the grid was observed and recorded. The anti-peeling rate of the coating was determined by calculating the ratio of the number of peeled grids to the total number of grids.

The chemical structures of Ti3C2Tx-PEG are shown in Fig. 2 A. The absorption peak observed at 1642 cm−1 in the Ti3C2Tx-PEG spectrum corresponded to the stretching vibration of the –C=O bond within the Ti3C2Tx structure. [38] Concurrently, the absorption peaks at 2879 cm−1, 1462 cm−1, and 1346 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of the –C–H bonds in the PEG polymer chain. [39] Additionally, the absorption peak at 1109 cm−1 correlated with the stretching vibration of the –C–O–C bond. The presence of these peaks indicates the successful synthesis of Ti3C2Tx-PEG. The XRD pattern of Ti3C2Tx exhibited two prominent peaks at approximately 2θ = 9.3° and 2θ = 19.0°, corresponding to the (002) and (004) crystal planes, respectively (Fig. 2B). The (002) peak is recognized as a typical feature of Ti3C2Tx with interlayer spacing measured at 9.5 Å. [40] The characteristic peak corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets was located at approximately 2θ = 7.2°, showing a slight angular shift. The calculated interlayer spacing was 12.3 Å, indicating an increase in the interlayer distance of Ti3C2Tx-PEG after DMSO intercalation and ultrasonic exfoliation treatment. Apart from the characteristic peaks of the (002) and (004) crystal planes, two new diffraction peaks were observed at approximately 2θ = 21.7° and 23.1°, which corresponded to the characteristic peaks of PEG. [41] The XRD spectrum also confirmed the successful fabrication of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets.

The morphology of Ti3C2Tx before and after modification was examined using SEM (Fig. S2). The untreated Ti3C2Tx exhibits an “accordion-like” multilayer structure with layers stacked in an orderly fashion, which is a typical characteristic of Ti3C2Tx material. After modification, the original multilayer structure of Ti3C2Tx was disrupted, and the nanosheets became thinner. Upon magnified observation (Fig. S2D), Ti3C2Tx-PEG displayed a wrinkled morphology. Since Ti3C2Tx nanosheets are materials at the nanoscale, SEM cannot clearly observe the internal structure of single-layer or few-layer Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets. Therefore, further characterization was performed using TEM, as shown in Fig. S3. The TEM images clearly show that Ti3C2Tx-PEG has been exfoliated into single-layer or few-layer structures. Upon magnification, the nanosheets are distinctly observed to be exfoliated into transparent thin layers (Fig. S3C), which is consistent with the expected results. These morphological changes, along with the emergence of optical transparency, collectively indicate the successful modification of Ti3C2Tx-PEG.

The zeta potentials of Ti3C2Tx and Ti3C2Tx-PEG were − 39.1 and − 44.6 mV (Fig. 2C), respectively, demonstrating that Ti3C2Tx-PEG was more stable than Ti3C2Tx. The long chain structure of PEG can extend into the solution, effectively preventing aggregation and clumping of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, thus maintaining their good dispersion. Meanwhile, the hydrophilic protective layer formed by PEG on the nanosheets further enhances the stability of the surface charge. [42,43] The stability of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets before and after modification was observed in different media, including water (1 mg/mL), hydrogel prepolymer, and the final gel state (Fig. 2D). Ti3C2Tx exhibited substantial layering in all three media, whereas Ti3C2Tx-PEG exhibited no layering in any of these media, indicating that PEG modification enhanced the stability of the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets.
The optical photos of the PSM hydrogel films and columns are shown in Fig. 3 A. The PSM0 hydrogel was transparent, PSM0.50 appeared gray, PSM0.75 appeared dark gray, and PSM1.0 was uniform black.

As shown in Fig. 3B, examination of the freeze-dried PSM hydrogel morphology after swelling was conducted using SEM. The SiO2@N-CDs microspheres served as crosslinking points within the hydrogel network, [44] effectively enhancing the three-dimensional structure with their rigid properties. This reinforcement enabled the PSM0 hydrogel to maintain a relatively good structural morphology and exhibit uniform pore wall thickness. With the addition of Ti3C2Tx-PEG, the network structure pore walls of the ternary composite hydrogels, PSM0.25, PSM0.50, and PSM0.75, appeared thicker. Aggregation of SiO2@N-CDs microspheres was observed on the PSM0.25 and PSM0.50 hydrogels. When the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content was further increased in PSM0.75, wrinkles appeared on the hydrogel pore walls and Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanoparticles were visible. For the PSM1.0 hydrogel, the surface pore size noticeably decreased, and the pore density increased. The network structure appeared more regular compared to PSM0.25, PSM0.50, and PSM0.75. The Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets, with their high specific surface area and strong interfacial interactions, form crosslinking points or network structures within the hydrogel through interactions with PEG molecules. This increases the rigidity of the network and thickens the hydrogel's pore walls. Additionally, the uneven dispersion of Ti3C2Tx-PEG and SiO2@N-CDs leads to the aggregation of inorganic materials, resulting in an irregular porous structure and a rough surface on the hydrogel [43].

Given the fluorescent properties of SiO2@N-CDs, fluorescence tracking of SiO2@N-CDs was conducted using CLSM to observe the distribution of SiO2@N-CDs in the hydrogel after the addition of Ti3C2Tx-PEG. In the PSM0 hydrogel, the SiO2@N-CDs microsphere sizes were similar, and their distribution was uniform and dense (Fig. 3C), indicating that SiO2@N-CDs was well dispersed in the hydrogel. With the addition of Ti3C2Tx-PEG, the dispersibility of the microspheres initially decreased and then increased. In the PSM0.25 hydrogel, large SiO2@N-CDs aggregates appeared with a relatively regular shape, and the number of SiO2@N-CDs decreased. In the PSM0.50 hydrogel, there was a sharp decrease in the number of SiO2@N-CDs, and the aggregation morphology became irregular. In the PSM0.75 hydrogel, there was a decrease in the quantity of SiO2@N-CDs, and no aggregation phenomenon was observed. The distribution was relatively uniform. The ternary composite hydrogel with a Ti3C2Tx-PEG content of 0.75 wt% exhibited good dispersibility.

The FTIR spectrum of the PSM hydrogel is shown in Fig. S4. The characteristic peak of –N=C=O at 2270 cm−1 disappeared, indicating the complete reaction of IPDI. [45] Absorption peaks of –N–H and –C=O stretching vibrations appeared at 3340 and 1711 cm−1, respectively. These changes indicate the formation of an aminoformate [9]. The stretching vibration peak of –C–O–C near 1092 cm−1 and the broad absorption peak of the methylene –C–H stretching vibration at ∼ 2920 cm−1 were observed. The characteristic peak observed at 1305 cm−1 corresponded to the –C–N stretching vibrations. These peaks indicate the successful synthesis of the hydrogel. [9,10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilicity and swelling properties of the PSM hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets enhanced the hydrophilicity of the PSM hydrogel. In the water contact angle data (Fig. S5A), the water contact angle of the PSM0 hydrogel was 57°, whereas the water contact angle of the PSM1.0 hydrogel was only 18.6°. The swelling degrees of the five types of PSM hydrogels were determined (Fig. S5B). With the introduction of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets into the hydrogel, the swelling degree of the hydrogel at equilibrium was higher than that of the PSM0 hydrogel without added nanosheets. Meanwhile, the water content of the PSM hydrogels were calculated (Fig. S5C). The water content of PSM0 hydrogel was 67.1%. The water content of the hydrogel increased as the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content increased, and the water content of PSM1.0 reaches 75.1%. Based on the chemical structure of the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets, it is evident that the PEG segments played a critical role in the hydrophilicity and swelling of PSM. PEG segments exhibit excellent hydrophilicity, effectively enhancing the interaction between the hydrogel and water molecules [46]. Additionally, the introduction of PEG segments provided more water absorption space for the hydrogel network, allowing the hydrogel to swell to a larger volume after water absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of the PSM hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Ti3C2Tx-PEG and SiO2@N-CDs act as crosslinking points, enhancing the mechanical properties of the antifouling PSM hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the PSM0.75 antifouling hydrogel could easily withstand various mechanical deformations, such as twisting, knotting, and stretching, demonstrating its excellent flexibility and toughness. Additionally, it could lift a dumbbell weighing up to 2.5 kg without rupturing, demonstrating its outstanding load-bearing capacity.

The stress–strain curves (Fig. 4B) of the PSM hydrogel showed that as the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content increased from 0.25 wt% to 0.75 wt%, the ternary composite hydrogels (PSM0.25, PSM0.50, and PSM0.75) exhibited an increasing trend in strain. Furthermore, their stress demonstrated a trend of initially increasing and then decreasing. However, even in the decreasing state, the stress remained higher than that of the original PSM0 hydrogel. The fracture strength and elongation at break of the antifouling PSM hydrogel are depicted in Fig. S6A. The fracture strength of the PSM0 hydrogel was 1.2 MPa, with an elongation at a break of 894%. As the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content increased, the fracture strength of the PSM hydrogel exhibited a decreasing trend, whereas the elongation at break continued to rise. The fracture strength of PSM0.75 was 1.62 MPa, and the elongation at break reached 1734%. The fracture strength of the PSM1.0 hydrogel was the lowest among the five groups (1.1 MPa). However, its elongation at break reached the highest value of 2187%, which was 2.3 times that of the PSM0 hydrogel. The fracture strength of binary PSM1.0 was slightly lower than that of PSM0, whereas the fracture strengths of all three ternary composite hydrogels exceeded that of the binary composite hydrogel. This indicates a synergistic effect between Ti3C2Tx-PEG and SiO2@N-CDs, which enhanced the strength of the hydrogel, enabling it to resist external forces and prevent material fracture. From the elongation at break data, it can be observed that Ti3C2Tx-PEG plays a positive role in improving the hydrogel toughness.

The Young’s modulus values of the PSM hydrogels were calculated (Fig. 4D). With the increase in the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content, the Young’s modulus of the PSM hydrogels initially increased and then decreased. The Young’s modulus of PSM0.25 reached its maximum value of 0.14 MPa. This variation could be attributed to the synergistic effect of Ti3C2Tx-PEG and SiO2@N-CDs in the ternary composite hydrogel. The layered structure of Ti3C2Tx-PEG effectively dispersed and resisted stress. SiO2@N-CDs possess high rigidity, enhancing the connection and stability between the PSM layers. The synergistic effect of the components allowed the hydrogel to maintain shape and structural stability, thereby increasing its Young’s modulus. The tensile properties of PSM hydrogel were compared with MXene hydrogel reported in recent literature. [47–53] The results showed that the PSM hydrogel has a strain of up to 22, which significantly surpasses other known hydrogel materials, demonstrating excellent tensile performance (Fig. S6B).

The PSM hydrogel underwent a single load–unload test (Fig. S6C). All five types of hydrogels exhibited hysteresis loops during the loading–unloading process, indicating that the PSM hydrogel could effectively absorb and dissipate energy when subjected to external forces. After completing a single load–unload tensile test, different durations of rest were allowed, and the toughness recovery rates of the five types of PSM hydrogels were calculated (Fig. S6D). With the extension of the rest time, the toughness recovery rate increased. The PSM1.0 hydrogel exhibited the best toughness recovery ability, with a 47.4% recovery of its original toughness after a 30 min rest. The toughness recovery rate of the in the ternary composite hydrogel, PSM0.75, was 43.1%, second only to PSM1.0. In contrast, the PSM0 hydrogel had the poorest toughness recovery capability, recovering only 27.7% of its original toughness. This difference may stem from variances in the hydrogel network structure. The rigid SiO2@N-CDs microspheres and polymer chains combined to form the network framework. However, owing to a lack of sufficient elastic recovery mechanisms after unloading, the force could not effectively propagate throughout the entire network, preventing the hydrogel from returning to its initial state. This consequently impacted its toughness recovery capability [54].

The toughness retention rate was calculated after performing two load–unload cycles on the PSM hydrogel with a 10 min interval between each cycle (Fig. S6E). The toughness retention rate of the PSM1.0 hydrogel reached 54.3%, and the PSM0.75 hydrogel showed a toughness retention rate of 47.3%, far exceeding that of PSM0 (31.9%). This result indicates that Ti3C2Tx-PEG not only enhanced toughness but also substantially improved the toughness recovery capability of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties of the PSM hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the concept of environmentally friendly antifouling, one important direction in current antifouling coating research is utilizing ultraviolet light and sunlight to enhance the antifouling performance of coatings. Photocatalytic materials have been proven to exhibit excellent antibacterial effects underwater, while MXene materials have often been used as catalysts for photocatalytic materials. Recent studies have shown that Ti3C2Tx (MXene) materials can convert part of the absorbed light into thermal energy to kill bacteria, [55,56] and research demonstrated that Ti3C2Tx exhibits certain antibacterial capabilities even under dark conditions. [57] In view of the excellent photothermal performance of Ti3C2Tx, we further explored whether the PSM hydrogel coating has photothermal bactericidal ability, aiming to achieve more effective antifouling through a multi-mode antifouling mechanism. To investigate the photothermal conversion efficacy of the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheet-containing hydrogels, the photothermal behavior of the PSM hydrogels was studied under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation (Fig. 5 A and 5B).

Under continuous laser irradiation, the surface temperature of the PSM0 hydrogel did not change substantially, indicating that the PSM0 hydrogel did not possess a photothermal conversion effect. The other four types of hydrogels demonstrated sensitivity to light exposure. Owing to the lower amount of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets in the PSM0.25 hydrogel, the temperature increase phenomenon under light exposure was not substantial. The PSM0.50, PSM0.75, and PSM1.0 hydrogels exhibited similar rates of temperature increase. Fig. 5C shows the temperature changes of the PSM hydrogels under five consecutive laser irradiation cycles. After five complete cycles of laser irradiation, the heating rates of the PSM hydrogels remained almost unchanged, with minimal fluctuations in the peak temperature of only 0.4°C, and the fluctuation amplitude did not exceed 1%. This result indicates that the PSM hydrogel exhibited excellent stability and repeatability in the photothermal conversion process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling performance of the PSM hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria are a major component of marine biofouling. To explore whether the PSM hydrogel has a broad-spectrum antibacterial effect, the gram-positive bacterium S. aureus and the gram-negative bacterium E. coli were selected for testing. Fig. 6 A illustrates the advantages of the four hydrogels, PSM0.25, PSM0.50, PSM0.75, and PSM1.0, compared to PSM0 in terms of resistance to bacterial attachment. As the nanosheet content increased, the bacterial count on the surface of the PSM hydrogels decreased, with PSM1.0 exhibiting the best resistance to bacterial attachment. This result indicates that the antibacterial performance of the PSM hydrogels could be effectively enhanced by adjusting the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheet content. Furthermore, the PSM hydrogels exhibited excellent photothermal sterilization performance. Under NIR irradiation, the bacterial colonies on the surface of the hydrogels containing Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets decreased. The PSM hydrogels containing Ti3C2Tx-PEG generated a thermal effect under light exposure, leading to an increase in the surface temperature. When bacteria were exposed to this temperature, their cell structures were damaged, ultimately resulting in bacterial death. Fig. 6B and 6C depict the SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus on the PSM hydrogel after exposure to NIR irradiation. These images clearly show the damage to bacterial cell walls, further confirming this phenomenon. The adsorption rates of the two bacteria on the PSM hydrogel surfaces were calculated (Fig. 6C and 6D). Under dark conditions, the number of bacteria on the PSM hydrogel surfaces decreased in a stepwise manner with the increase in the Ti3C2Tx-PEG content. After exposure to NIR irradiation, the sterilization rate of E. coli by PSM0.75 reached 100%, and that of S. aureus reached 98.7%. The sterilization rate of both bacteria by PSM1.0 was 100%. The introduction of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets into PEG enhanced not only their dispersibility and stability in the hydrogel but also the antibacterial performance of PSM. The flexible chain and hydrophilic properties of PEG caused steric hindrance, [58] effectively inhibiting bacterial adhesion. Ti3C2Tx exhibited photothermal effects, which can be utilized to kill bacteria. This photothermal sterilization mechanism combined with the spatial effect antibacterial mechanism of PEG provides PSM with dual antibacterial protection.

After immersing the PSM hydrogel in seawater containing diatoms for 24 h, it was gently rinsed with sterile seawater. The surface chlorophyll-a concentration of the PSM hydrogel was measured to calculate its diatom adsorption rate (Fig. 6E and 6F). The concentration of diatom chlorophyll-a on the PSM0 hydrogel was 0.15 mg/L, with an adsorption rate of 25.8%. When the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheet content reached its maximum value of 1 wt%, the chlorophyll-a concentration of diatoms decreased to 0.07 mg/L and the corresponding adsorption rate decreased to 10.3%. This change indicates that the introduction of Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets enhanced the ability of the PSM hydrogel to resist diatom adhesion.

Further, the antifouling performance was tested via 180 days seawater immersion experiment. The iron plates were polished and coated with epoxy primer followed by a coating of PSM hydrogel. The immersion experiment was conducted using seawater from the Yellow Sea area of Dalian. As shown in Fig. 7 A, after 180 days of seawater immersion, the PSM0.75 surface had no visible fouling organisms and maintained an intact morphology. The soaked coatings were tested to evaluate their resistance to marine bacterial adhesion (Fig. 7B) and benthic diatom adhesion (Fig. 7C). After 180 days, the bacterial adhesion rate (BAR) on PSM0 was significantly higher than that of the other hydrogels. Compared to PSM0, the PSM0.25, PSM0.50, PSM0.75, and PSM1.0 showed significant advantages in both bacterial attachment resistance and benthic diatom attachment resistance. As the Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheet content increased, the adhesion of bacteria and diatoms to the PSM hydrogel initially decreased and then increased.

By testing the interlayer adhesion strength of the coatings and comparing it with the antifouling performance. The results indicate that the higher the anti-peeling rate (APR), the stronger the interlayer adhesion, and the better the antifouling effect (Fig. 7D). The PSM0.75 hydrogel, maintained its integrity with no visible damage or detachment on the surface. This structure ensures that the hydrogel’s hydration layer and the photothermal synergistic bactericidal properties continue to function steadily. In contrast, other coatings, after prolonged exposure to the seawater environment, suffered structural damage, leading to a decline in antifouling performance. Once these coatings detached, their antifouling capability was completely lost.

The relationship between the hydrophilicity and antifouling performance of PSM hydrogels was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 8 A, there is a positive correlation between the hydrophilicity and antifouling performance of PSM hydrogels. With the increase in Ti3C2Tx-PEG content, the hydrophilicity and antifouling performance both improve. The enhanced hydrophilicity of PSM hydrogels mainly stems from the increased PEG content. PEG not only has excellent hydrophilicity but also forms a hydration layer structure with water molecules through hydrogen bonding, utilizing spatial repulsion to resist the adhesion of biofouling. The relationship between the photothermal properties and antifouling performance of PSM hydrogels was also analyzed.

As shown in Fig. 8B the higher the photothermal temperature of the PSM hydrogels, the better the antifouling effect. This is because Ti3C2Tx can efficiently absorb light and convert it into heat, while generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) free radicals that kill the bacteria attached to its surface. [57] Under visible light irradiation, Ti3C2Tx material can rapidly heat up and reach a stable temperature within approximately 200 s. [57] The PSM hydrogel coating with Ti3C2Tx also exhibits the photothermal properties. When the hydrogel coating absorbs light energy, its temperature increases, killing a portion of the bacteria attached to the surface. Meanwhile, the generated ROS free radicals interact with the microorganisms, further leading to cell inactivation and death. The antifouling mechanism of PSM hydrogels is illustrated in Fig. 8C. PSM hydrogels enhance hydrophilicity through Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets, form a PEG hydration layer structure to create an antifouling barrier, and ultimately achieve efficient antifouling through the synergistic effect of photothermal sterilization.

We have proposed a strategy to enhance the toughness of hydrogels using Ti3C2Tx-PEG. Initially, by combining inorganic Ti3C2Tx with organic PEG, we successfully obtained Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets with excellent stability in hydrogels. PSM hydrogels were then synthesized by combining Ti3C2Tx-PEG nanosheets with SiO2@N-CDs microspheres, resulting in substantially improved hydrophilicity and swelling properties. The hydrogel with a Ti3C2Tx-PEG content of 0.75 wt% exhibited outstanding mechanical properties, including a fracture strength of 1.62 MPa, elongation at break of 1734%, and toughness of 17.7 MJ/m3. Additionally, the PSM hydrogel inherited the photothermal properties of Ti3C2Tx, displaying excellent photothermal antibacterial performance. Moreover, the PEG chains on Ti3C2Tx-PEG enhanced the antifouling properties of the PSM hydrogel, synergizing with the photothermal sterilization effect of Ti3C2Tx, thus broadening its application prospects in the field of antifouling.

Miaomiao Ma conducted the research design and data analysis, and wrote and revised the paper; Qi’an Chen assisted with the SEM testing; Zhanping Zhang and Yuhong Qi provided expert guidance and revisions to the paper.
Miaomiao Ma: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuhong Qi: Supervision. Qi’an Chen: Visualization. Zhanping Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
